population: Adults, children, and young people with established coeliac disease, preserving age-specific monitoring and growth context.
intended_setting: Primary, gastroenterology, hepatology, colorectal, result-review, or outpatient follow-up as qualified by the exact source.

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: People have the right to be involved in discussions and make informed decisions about their care, as described in NICE's information on making decisions about your care . Making decisions using NICE guidelines explains how we use words to show the strength (or certainty) of our recommendations, and has information about prescribing medicines (including off-label use), professional guidelines, standards and laws (including on consent and mental capacity), and safeguarding.

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Offer serological testing for coeliac disease to: people with any of the following: persistent unexplained abdominal or gastrointestinal symptoms faltering growth prolonged fatigue unexpected weight loss severe or persistent mouth ulcers unexplained iron, vitamin B12 or folate deficiency type 1 diabetes, at diagnosis autoimmune thyroid disease, at diagnosis irritable bowel syndrome (in adults) first‑degree relatives of people with coeliac disease.

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Offer serological testing for coeliac disease to: people with any of the following: persistent unexplained abdominal or gastrointestinal symptoms faltering growth prolonged fatigue unexpected weight loss severe or persistent mouth ulcers unexplained iron, vitamin B12 or folate deficiency type 1 diabetes, at diagnosis autoimmune thyroid disease, at diagnosis irritable bowel syndrome (in adults) fir

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management

evidence_statements.population: Adults, young people, and children with suspected or confirmed coeliac disease, with age-specific testing and referral recommendations.
evidence_statements.setting: Primary and specialist care as qualified by the cited recommendations.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: England and Wales; international evidence requiring UAE pathway adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Consider serological testing for coeliac disease in people with any of the following: metabolic bone disorder (reduced bone mineral density or osteomalacia) unexplained neurological symptoms (particularly peripheral neuropathy or ataxia) unexplained subfertility or recurrent miscarriage persistently raised liver enzymes with unknown cause dental enamel defects Down's syndrome Turner syndrome.
evidence_statements.source_title: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management — Recommendations

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Consider serological testing for coeliac disease in people with any of the following: metabolic bone disorder (reduced bone mineral density or osteomalacia) unexplained neurological symptoms (particularly peripheral neuropathy or ataxia) unexplained subfertility or recurrent miscarriage persistently raised liver enzymes with unknown cause dental enamel defects Down's syndrome Turner syndrome.

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management

evidence_statements.population: Adults, young people, and children with suspected or confirmed coeliac disease, with age-specific testing and referral recommendations.
evidence_statements.setting: Primary and specialist care as qualified by the cited recommendations.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: England and Wales; international evidence requiring UAE pathway adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: For people undergoing investigations for coeliac disease: explain that any test is accurate only if a gluten‑containing diet is eaten during the diagnostic process and advise the person not to start a gluten‑free diet until diagnosis is confirmed by a specialist, even if the results of a serological test are positive.
evidence_statements.source_title: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management — Recommendations

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: For people undergoing investigations for coeliac disease: explain that any test is accurate only if a gluten‑containing diet is eaten during the diagnostic process and advise the person not to start a gluten‑free diet until diagnosis is confirmed by a specialist, even if the results of a serological test are positive.

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management

evidence_statements.population: Adults, young people, and children with suspected or confirmed coeliac disease, with age-specific testing and referral recommendations.
evidence_statements.setting: Primary and specialist care as qualified by the cited recommendations.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: England and Wales; international evidence requiring UAE pathway adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Advise people who are following a normal diet (containing gluten) to eat some gluten in more than 1 meal every day for at least 6 weeks before testing.
evidence_statements.source_title: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management — Recommendations

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Advise people who are following a normal diet (containing gluten) to eat some gluten in more than 1 meal every day for at least 6 weeks before testing.

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management

evidence_statements.population: Adults, young people, and children with suspected or confirmed coeliac disease, with age-specific testing and referral recommendations.
evidence_statements.setting: Primary and specialist care as qualified by the cited recommendations.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: England and Wales; international evidence requiring UAE pathway adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: If people who have restricted their gluten intake or excluded gluten from their diet are reluctant or unable to re‑introduce gluten into their diet before testing: refer the person to a gastrointestinal specialist and explain that it may be difficult to confirm their diagnosis by intestinal biopsy.
evidence_statements.source_title: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management — Recommendations

exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: If people who have restricted their gluten intake or excluded gluten from their diet are reluctant or unable to re‑introduce gluten into their diet before testing: refer the person to a gastrointestinal specialist and explain that it may be difficult to confirm their diagnosis by intestinal biopsy.

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management

evidence_statements.population: Adults, young people, and children with suspected or confirmed coeliac disease, with age-specific testing and referral recommendations.
evidence_statements.setting: Primary and specialist care as qualified by the cited recommendations.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: England and Wales; international evidence requiring UAE pathway adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Advise people who have tested negative for coeliac disease, particularly first‑degree relatives and people with type 1 diabetes, that: coeliac disease may present with a wide range of symptoms and they should consult their healthcare professional if any of the symptoms listed in recommendations 1.1.1 or 1.1.2 arise or persist.

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: All serological tests should be undertaken in laboratories with clinical pathology accreditation (CPA) or ISO15189 accreditation.
evidence_statements.source_title: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management — Recommendations

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: All serological tests should be undertaken in laboratories with clinical pathology accreditation (CPA) or ISO15189 accreditation.

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management

evidence_statements.population: Adults, young people, and children with suspected or confirmed coeliac disease, with age-specific testing and referral recommendations.
evidence_statements.setting: Primary and specialist care as qualified by the cited recommendations.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: England and Wales; international evidence requiring UAE pathway adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: When healthcare professionals request serological tests to investigate suspected coeliac disease in young people and adults, laboratories should: test for total immunoglobulin A (IgA) and IgA tissue transglutaminase (tTG) as the first choice use IgA endomysial antibodies (EMA) if IgA tTG is weakly positive consider using IgG EMA, IgG deamidated gliadin peptide (DGP) or IgG tTG if IgA is deficient .
evidence_statements.source_title: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management — Recommendations

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: When healthcare professionals request serological tests to investigate suspected coeliac disease in young people and adults, laboratories should: test for total immunoglobulin A (IgA) and IgA tissue transglutaminase (tTG) as the first choice use IgA endomysial antibodies (EMA) if IgA tTG is weakly positive consider using IgG EMA, IgG deamidated gliadin peptide (DGP) or IgG tTG if IgA is deficient

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Do not use human leukocyte antigen (HLA) DQ2 (DQ2.2 and DQ2.5)/DQ8 testing in the initial diagnosis of coeliac disease in non‑specialist settings.

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management

evidence_statements.population: Adults, young people, and children with suspected or confirmed coeliac disease, with age-specific testing and referral recommendations.
evidence_statements.setting: Primary and specialist care as qualified by the cited recommendations.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: England and Wales; international evidence requiring UAE pathway adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Only consider using HLA DQ2 (DQ2.2 and DQ2.5)/DQ8 testing in the diagnosis of coeliac disease in specialist settings (for example, in children who are not having a biopsy, or in people who already have limited gluten ingestion and choose not to have a gluten challenge).

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Laboratories should clearly communicate the interpretation of serological test results and recommended action to healthcare professionals.

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management

evidence_statements.population: Adults, young people, and children with suspected or confirmed coeliac disease, with age-specific testing and referral recommendations.
evidence_statements.setting: Primary and specialist care as qualified by the cited recommendations.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: England and Wales; international evidence requiring UAE pathway adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Refer young people and adults with positive serological test results to a gastrointestinal specialist for endoscopic intestinal biopsy to confirm or exclude coeliac disease. [In young people and adults, a positive serological test result is defined as unambiguously positive IgA tTG alone, or weakly positive IgA tTG and a positive IgA EMA test result. Note: In people who have IgA deficiency, a serologically positive result can be derived from any one of the IgG antibodies].
evidence_statements.source_title: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management — Recommendations

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
1.3 Referral of people with suspected coeliac disease
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Refer young people and adults with positive serological test results to a gastrointestinal specialist for endoscopic intestinal biopsy to confirm or exclude coeliac disease. [In young people and adults, a positive serological test result is defined as unambiguously positive IgA tTG alone, or weakly positive IgA tTG and a positive IgA EMA test result. Note: In people who have IgA deficiency, a sero

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
1.3 Referral of people with suspected coeliac disease

evidence_statements.population: Adults, young people, and children with suspected or confirmed coeliac disease, with age-specific testing and referral recommendations.
evidence_statements.setting: Primary and specialist care as qualified by the cited recommendations.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: England and Wales; international evidence requiring UAE pathway adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Refer children with positive serological test results to a paediatric gastroenterologist or paediatrician with a specialist interest in gastroenterology for further investigation for coeliac disease. [Further investigation may include, but is not limited to, one or more of the following: an IgA EMA test to confirm serological positivity, HLA genetic testing, an endoscopic biopsy].
evidence_statements.source_title: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management — Recommendations

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management

[Further investigation may include, but is not limited to, one or more of the following: an IgA EMA test to confirm serological positivity, HLA genetic testing, an endoscopic biopsy].

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
1.3 Referral of people with suspected coeliac disease

evidence_statements.jurisdiction: England and Wales; international evidence requiring UAE pathway adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Refer people with negative serological test results to a gastrointestinal specialist for further assessment if coeliac disease is still clinically suspected.

evidence_statements.setting: Primary and specialist care as qualified by the cited recommendations.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: England and Wales; international evidence requiring UAE pathway adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Healthcare professionals should have a low threshold for re‑testing people identified in recommendations 1.1.1 or 1.1.2 if they develop any symptoms consistent with coeliac disease.
evidence_statements.source_title: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management — Recommendations

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
1.3 Referral of people with suspected coeliac disease
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Healthcare professionals should have a low threshold for re‑testing people identified in recommendations 1.1.1 or 1.1.2 if they develop any symptoms consistent with coeliac disease.

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
1.3 Referral of people with suspected coeliac disease

evidence_statements.population: Adults, young people, and children with suspected or confirmed coeliac disease, with age-specific testing and referral recommendations.
evidence_statements.setting: Primary and specialist care as qualified by the cited recommendations.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: England and Wales; international evidence requiring UAE pathway adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Consider referring people with coeliac disease for endoscopic intestinal biopsy if continued exposure to gluten has been excluded and: serological titres are persistently high and show little or no change after 12 months or they have persistent symptoms, including diarrhoea, abdominal pain, weight loss, fatigue or unexplained anaemia.
evidence_statements.source_title: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management — Recommendations

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
1.4 Monitoring in people with coeliac disease
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Consider referring people with coeliac disease for endoscopic intestinal biopsy if continued exposure to gluten has been excluded and: serological titres are persistently high and show little or no change after 12 months or they have persistent symptoms, including diarrhoea, abdominal pain, weight loss, fatigue or unexplained anaemia.

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
1.4 Monitoring in people with coeliac disease

evidence_statements.population: Adults, young people, and children with suspected or confirmed coeliac disease, with age-specific testing and referral recommendations.
evidence_statements.setting: Primary and specialist care as qualified by the cited recommendations.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: England and Wales; international evidence requiring UAE pathway adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Do not use serological testing alone to determine whether gluten has been excluded from the person's diet.
evidence_statements.source_title: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management — Recommendations

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
1.4 Monitoring in people with coeliac disease
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Do not use serological testing alone to determine whether gluten has been excluded from the person's diet.

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
1.4 Monitoring in people with coeliac disease

evidence_statements.population: Adults, young people, and children with suspected or confirmed coeliac disease, with age-specific testing and referral recommendations.
evidence_statements.setting: Primary and specialist care as qualified by the cited recommendations.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: England and Wales; international evidence requiring UAE pathway adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Offer an annual review to people with coeliac disease. During the review: measure weight and height review symptoms consider the need for assessment of diet and adherence to the gluten‑free diet consider the need for specialist dietetic and nutritional advice.
evidence_statements.source_title: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management — Recommendations

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
1.4 Monitoring in people with coeliac disease
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Offer an annual review to people with coeliac disease. During the review: measure weight and height review symptoms consider the need for assessment of diet and adherence to the gluten‑free diet consider the need for specialist dietetic and nutritional advice.

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
1.4 Monitoring in people with coeliac disease

evidence_statements.population: Adults, young people, and children with suspected or confirmed coeliac disease, with age-specific testing and referral recommendations.
evidence_statements.setting: Primary and specialist care as qualified by the cited recommendations.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: England and Wales; international evidence requiring UAE pathway adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Refer the person to a GP or consultant if concerns are raised in the annual review. The GP or consultant should assess all of the following: the need for a dual‑energy X‑ray absorptiometry (DEXA) scan (in line with the NICE guideline on osteoporosis: assessing the risk of fragility fracture ) or active treatment of bone disease the need for specific blood tests the risk of long‑term complications and comorbidities the need for specialist referral.
evidence_statements.source_title: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management — Recommendations

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
1.4 Monitoring in people with coeliac disease
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Refer the person to a GP or consultant if concerns are raised in the annual review. The GP or consultant should assess all of the following: the need for a dual‑energy X‑ray absorptiometry (DEXA) scan (in line with the NICE guideline on osteoporosis: assessing the risk of fragility fracture ) or active treatment of bone disease the need for specific blood tests the risk of long‑term complications

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
1.4 Monitoring in people with coeliac disease

evidence_statements.population: Adults, young people, and children with suspected or confirmed coeliac disease, with age-specific testing and referral recommendations.
evidence_statements.setting: Primary and specialist care as qualified by the cited recommendations.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: England and Wales; international evidence requiring UAE pathway adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Consider the following actions in people with coeliac disease who have persistent symptoms despite advice to exclude gluten from their diet: review the certainty of the original diagnosis refer the person to a specialist dietitian to investigate continued exposure to gluten investigate potential complications or coexisting conditions that may be causing persistent symptoms, such as irritable bowel syndrome, lactose intolerance, bacterial overgrowth, microscopic colitis or inflammatory colitis.
evidence_statements.source_title: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management — Recommendations

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Consider the following actions in people with coeliac disease who have persistent symptoms despite advice to exclude gluten from their diet: review the certainty of the original diagnosis refer the person to a specialist dietitian to investigate continued exposure to gluten investigate potential complications or coexisting conditions that may be causing persistent symptoms, such as irritable bowel

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management

evidence_statements.population: Adults, young people, and children with suspected or confirmed coeliac disease, with age-specific testing and referral recommendations.
evidence_statements.setting: Primary and specialist care as qualified by the cited recommendations.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: England and Wales; international evidence requiring UAE pathway adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Diagnose refractory coeliac disease if the original diagnosis of coeliac disease has been confirmed, and exposure to gluten and any coexisting conditions have been excluded as the cause of continuing symptoms.
evidence_statements.source_title: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management — Recommendations

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Diagnose refractory coeliac disease if the original diagnosis of coeliac disease has been confirmed, and exposure to gluten and any coexisting conditions have been excluded as the cause of continuing symptoms.

evidence_statements.setting: Primary and specialist care as qualified by the cited recommendations.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: England and Wales; international evidence requiring UAE pathway adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Refer people with refractory coeliac disease to a specialist centre for further investigation.
evidence_statements.source_title: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management — Recommendations

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Refer people with refractory coeliac disease to a specialist centre for further investigation.

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management

evidence_statements.population: Adults, young people, and children with suspected or confirmed coeliac disease, with age-specific testing and referral recommendations.
evidence_statements.setting: Primary and specialist care as qualified by the cited recommendations.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: England and Wales; international evidence requiring UAE pathway adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Consider prednisolone for the initial management of the symptoms of refractory coeliac disease in adults while waiting for specialist advice. See recommendations on managing glucocorticoid withdrawal in NICE's guideline on adrenal insufficiency .
evidence_statements.source_title: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management — Recommendations

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Consider prednisolone for the initial management of the symptoms of refractory coeliac disease in adults while waiting for specialist advice. See recommendations on managing glucocorticoid withdrawal in NICE's guideline on adrenal insufficiency .

evidence_statements.population: Adults, young people, and children with suspected or confirmed coeliac disease, with age-specific testing and referral recommendations.
evidence_statements.setting: Primary and specialist care as qualified by the cited recommendations.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: England and Wales; international evidence requiring UAE pathway adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Explain to people who are thought to be at risk of coeliac disease that a delayed diagnosis, or undiagnosed coeliac disease, can result in continuing ill health and serious long‑term complications.
evidence_statements.source_title: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management — Recommendations

exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Explain to people who are thought to be at risk of coeliac disease that a delayed diagnosis, or undiagnosed coeliac disease, can result in continuing ill health and serious long‑term complications.

evidence_statements.jurisdiction: England and Wales; international evidence requiring UAE pathway adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Give people with coeliac disease (and their family members or carers, where appropriate) sources of information on the disease, including national and local specialist coeliac groups and dietitians with a specialist knowledge in coeliac disease.

exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Give people with coeliac disease (and their family members or carers, where appropriate) sources of information on the disease, including national and local specialist coeliac groups and dietitians with a specialist knowledge in coeliac disease.

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management

evidence_statements.population: Adults, young people, and children with suspected or confirmed coeliac disease, with age-specific testing and referral recommendations.
evidence_statements.setting: Primary and specialist care as qualified by the cited recommendations.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: England and Wales; international evidence requiring UAE pathway adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: A healthcare professional with a specialist knowledge of coeliac disease should tell people with a confirmed diagnosis of coeliac disease (and their family members or carers, where appropriate) about the importance of a gluten‑free diet and give them information to help them follow it. This should include: information on which types of food contain gluten and suitable alternatives, including gluten‑free substitutes explanations of food labelling information sources about gluten‑free diets, recipe ideas and cookbooks how to manage social situations, eating out and travelling away from home, including travel abroad avoiding cross contamination in the home and minimising the risk of accidental gluten intake when eating out the role of national and local coeliac support groups.
evidence_statements.source_title: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management — Recommendations

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Be aware that people with coeliac disease may experience anxiety and depression. Diagnose and manage these issues in line with the following NICE guidelines: Depression in adults with a chronic physical health problem Depression in children and young people Generalised anxiety disorder and panic disorder (with or without agoraphobia) in adults Social anxiety disorder .

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Explain to people with coeliac disease and their family members or carers (as appropriate) that the Department of Health's Green Book recommends immunisation against pneumococcus.

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Advise people with coeliac disease (and their family members or carers, where appropriate) to seek advice from a member of their healthcare team if they are thinking about taking over‑the‑counter vitamin or mineral supplements.

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Explain to people with coeliac disease (and their family members or carers, where appropriate) that they may need to take specific supplements such as calcium or vitamin D if their dietary intake is insufficient.

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Explain to people with coeliac disease (and their family members or carers, where appropriate) that: they can choose to include gluten‑free oats in their diet at any stage and they will be advised whether to continue eating gluten‑free oats depending on their immunological, clinical or histological response.
